spec_match.rationale: The JavaScript checks correctly target key spec entries: HTMLAnchorElement.attributionSrc, Request.attributionReporting, and navigator.permissions queries for attribution-reporting.

interactive.rationale: Performs real-time, dynamic feature-detection checks in the current browser and displays live 'still present' vs 'removed' badges.

blurb_alignment.rationale: Perfect alignment with parent index: queries headers, initialization objects, anchors, and Permissions Policy interfaces.

edge_cases.rationale: Uses robust try-catch blocks when executing Permissions queries and Request constructors, avoiding script crashes if APIs are already missing.

references.rationale: Includes direct on-page references linking to the parent feature index and ChromeStatus entry.

design_system.rationale: Conforms perfectly to colors, margins, and table styles specified in the design system.

runtime_evidence.rationale: Fresh Chrome DevTools MCP v152 sweep loaded the copied concept, exercised every detected control, found named controls, and reported no post-triage runtime/action failure.

conformance.rationale: A v152 suite now carries the same feature-ID/spec contract without modifying the immutable source-milestone suite.

openQuestions.title: Add explanations of what each surface did
openQuestions.detail: The list of surfaces is excellent but very brief. We could expand the table (or add toggle drawers) to explain what each surface (like Request.attributionReporting) did and what it's used for, giving developers studying deprecation impact more context.

summary: A clean, highly compliant capability-probing concept. By executing real-time browser diagnostics with try-catch safety nets, it lets developers accurately see which parts of the deprecated Attribution Reporting API are still active in their browser. Minor suggestion to add context explaining the role of each surface.
